title: Chapter 1: Game Over
content: The screaming started at 9:47 AM.

I know the exact time because I'd just finished a forty-minute boss fight in *Elden Ring: Resurgence* and was screenshotting my victory stats when the first shriek cut through my headphones like a blade. I pulled them off, annoyed—my upstairs neighbor Maria had a habit of screaming at her soap operas, and Wednesday was her marathon day.
But this wasn't a television scream.
This was the sound a human throat makes when it's being torn open.

I froze. The screaming multiplied—first one voice, then three, then a dozen, rising through the walls and floor like heat from a fire. Something heavy crashed in the hallway outside my apartment. Then a wet, ripping sound that I'd heard a thousand times in video games but never, ever in real life.
My hands were still on the keyboard. The victory screen glowed: **GREAT DEMI-GOD FELLED**.
"Funny," I whispered.

The power died at 10:15. My monitor went black, my mini-fridge hummed into silence, and the air conditioning that made my twelfth-floor apartment livable in July stopped blowing. I sat in the sudden darkness of Apartment 12B, Crimson Ridge Complex, listening to the sounds of the world ending.

I'm not going to pretend I was brave. I'm Alex Chen, twenty-four years old, professional game streamer with exactly forty-three subscribers on my best day. My greatest physical achievement was a 5K I walked in 2019 because my mom signed us up. I had three cans of tuna, two liters of water, a dead laptop, and a smartphone that couldn't find a signal.
What I had going for me was this: I was a coward who never left his apartment.

While the rest of the world had been out living their lives—going to work, eating at restaurants, riding the subway, breathing recirculated air in office buildings—I'd been right here, in my gaming chair, sealed behind a steel-reinforced door that I'd installed because the courier kept "accidentally" opening my packages.
I hadn't been exposed. I hadn't been touched.
I was alive because I had no life.

By noon, I'd pieced together enough from the fading emergency broadcasts on my battery radio to understand the shape of the catastrophe. A virus—fast-acting, airborne in its initial wave, transmitted through bodily fluids after that. It turned people into something between rabid and dead within hours. The CDC alert called them "the Infected." The panicked radio host who signed off mid-sentence called them what everyone else would soon call them:
Zombies.
I know. I laughed too. Then I looked through my peephole, and I stopped laughing.

The hallway of the twelfth floor looked like a crime scene painted by a madman. Two of my neighbors—Mr. Patterson from 12A and the quiet woman from 12C whose name I'd never learned—were shambling down the corridor. Mr. Patterson's left arm hung at an angle that arms don't hang. The woman's mouth was permanently open, jaw dislocated, teeth black with something I didn't want to identify.
They weren't the worst of it.
The worst was the girl standing perfectly still outside Apartment 12D.

She was—had been—beautiful. Asian, maybe mid-twenties, long black hair that fell past her shoulders in a curtain. She wore a silk camisole and shorts, like she'd been getting ready for bed when it hit her. Her skin was pale but intact—not the gray-green decay I could see on Mr. Patterson's face. Her eyes were open, fixed on nothing, pupils dilated to black discs.
She wasn't shambling. She wasn't moaning. She was just standing there, as if she'd stepped out for the mail and forgotten what she was doing.
I watched her for ten minutes through the peephole. She didn't move.
"Okay," I said to my empty apartment. "Okay. Okay. Okay."
I said it a lot. It didn't help.
That was when my wrist started burning.
I thought it was a rash at first—stress reaction, maybe. Then I looked down and saw the watch.

It hadn't been there before. It was silver, slim, with a face that glowed a soft blue-white. The band was fused to my skin, not painfully but seamlessly, as if it had grown there. I pulled at it. Nothing. I grabbed a butter knife from the kitchen and tried to pry it off. The watch didn't budge, and the knife bent.
The screen flickered.

**ROMANCE SYSTEM v1.0**
**HOST DETECTED: ALEX CHEN**
**STATUS: ACTIVATED**
**CURRENT LEVEL: 1 — NOVICE**
I stared. Then I laughed—not the good kind of laugh. The kind that comes right before crying.
"This is a hallucination," I told myself. "Stress hallucination. Classic."
The watch buzzed again.

**ACTIVE QUEST: FIRST ROMANCE**
**TARGET: Female, Age 26, Located 4 meters from your position**
**DIFFICULTY: ★★☆☆☆**
**CONDITION: Make physical contact with the target and speak to her with genuine emotional intent**
**REWARD: Fresh Raw Meat (2kg) + Purified Water (5L)**
**WARNING: The Hollow Heart Protocol is active. Transactional interactions will be penalized.**
I looked through the peephole again. The beautiful zombie girl was still standing there, motionless, staring at nothing.
The watch wanted me to romance a zombie.
"I've lost my mind," I said. "The virus is airborne. I'm already turning. This is the fever dream before I—"
**QUEST TIMER: 23:59:47**
A countdown. I had twenty-four hours to make contact with the most dangerous creature on the planet—the walking dead—or... what? The quest failed? The system shut down? I died?

I didn't know. But the countdown was real, burning on my wrist in blue-white light, and so was the hunger gnawing at my stomach, and so were the three cans of tuna that represented my entire food supply in a world that had stopped making food.
I pressed my forehead against the door and stared at the peephole.
The zombie girl outside 12D still hadn't moved.
Twenty-three hours, fifty-two minutes.
"I'm going to die," I whispered.
Then, quieter: "Or I'm going to do something insane."
My hand went to the door lock.
I hesitated.
The screaming had stopped in the hallway. Either everyone was dead, or everyone was like the girl—standing still in the middle of the apocalypse, waiting for something.
Waiting for me.
I unlocked the door.
